authoritative_sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual, 518210 Computing Infrastructure Providers, Data Processing, Web Hosting, and Related Services
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Census Bureau and Office of Management and Budget
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The NAICS manual is the official U.S. industry classification baseline. NAICS 518210 covers computing infrastructure, cloud computing, web hosting, data processing, cloud storage, and media streaming technical support services relevant to SaaS hosted-service operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Named industry boundary, hosted-service vocabulary, streaming support, cloud infrastructure, and service-context classification
used_for: SaaS, web-hosting, cloud, data-processing, and streaming-support industry boundary
distinguishing hosted-service operations from software publishing, engineering consulting, and generic support
artifact vocabulary for computing infrastructure, cloud storage, data processing, and media streaming support

authoritative_sources.title: ISO/IEC 20000-1:2018 Information technology - Service management
authoritative_sources.publisher: International Organization for Standardization / International Electrotechnical Commission
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ISO/IEC 20000-1 is a recognized international service-management system standard for planning, operating, monitoring, reviewing, and improving technology services, making it authoritative for SaaS operations lifecycle, fulfillment, service-level, supplier, incident, and change discipline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Service request intake, lifecycle progression, change and incident coordination, service-level handling, supplier dependency, evidence review, and continual-improvement handoff
used_for: lifecycle-state progression and hold/rework/complete gates
service-level, incident, change, supplier, and fulfillment terminology
completion evidence and review discipline

authoritative_sources.title: ITIL 4 Practice: Service Request Management
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official ITIL practice guidance is recognized for service request fulfillment, request models, communication, automation, and escalation. It directly supports the SaaS operations queue behavior for accepted, standard, and exception-driven fulfillment work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Service request classification, runbook use, fulfillment progression, stakeholder communication, and escalation routing
used_for: request fulfillment and exception queue terminology
safe customer or stakeholder communication boundaries
service request versus incident or change escalation

authoritative_sources.title: TMF621 Trouble Ticket Management API
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TM Forum Open APIs are recognized telecom and digital-service interface standards. TMF621 defines trouble-ticket records, related parties, related entities, status, and update patterns used in hosted-service, carrier, and connectivity handoffs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Trouble-ticket evidence, carrier or provider handoff, incident-adjacent status, and operational update records
used_for: ticket status and handoff artifacts
provider and carrier escalation package contents
source-record discipline for NOC and hosted-service exceptions

authoritative_sources.title: TMF641 Service Ordering Management API
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TMF641 is a recognized public service-order specification for requested service changes, order state, dependencies, related parties, and completion documentation in telecom, cloud-connectivity, and hosted-service workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Service-order intake, order-state progression, dependency tracking, fulfillment evidence, and completion hold/rework gates
used_for: service order and dependency vocabulary
state progression and completion evidence expectations
refusal when order state and operational evidence conflict

authoritative_sources.title: MEF 48.1 Service Activation Testing
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: MEF is a recognized standards body for carrier Ethernet and connectivity services. Service activation testing provides practical evidence expectations for activating connectivity dependencies that support SaaS environments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Connectivity activation test evidence, turn-up validation, carrier dependency, and service acceptance support
used_for: activation-test evidence before claiming connectivity readiness
cloud on-ramp and cross-connect completion conditions
handoff to network, carrier, or site operations when activation evidence fails

authoritative_sources.title: SP 800-61 Rev. 3, Incident Response Recommendations and Considerations for Cybersecurity Risk Management
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Official NIST incident-response guidance is authoritative for preparation, detection, response, recovery, evidence preservation, and controlled information sharing when SaaS operations intersects security incidents.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Security-sensitive classification, incident-owner escalation, evidence preservation, and information-sharing constraints
used_for: security incident escalation triggers
support versus incident-command boundary
refusal to disclose sensitive incident data without authorization

authoritative_sources.title: SP 800-53 Rev. 5, Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is an authoritative public control catalog for access control, audit and accountability, configuration, incident response, privacy, and system integrity. It grounds SaaS operations source-system, audit-log, access, and control-evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Access control, audit logging, configuration evidence, privacy, system integrity, and source-system dependency review
used_for: audit and accountability evidence
access and confidentiality constraints
refusal when controlled records or logs cannot be safely retrieved

authoritative_sources.title: NIST Privacy Framework
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST's Privacy Framework is an official privacy-risk management framework and is authoritative for customer, subscriber, tenant, log, content, and personal-data handling constraints in SaaS operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Data minimization, confidentiality screening, privacy-sensitive handoff, authorized recipient review, and disclosure refusal
used_for: privacy and confidentiality gatekeeping
unapproved recipient or channel refusal
privacy-rights and personal-data escalation triggers

authoritative_sources.title: Cloud Controls Matrix
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The CSA Cloud Controls Matrix is a recognized cloud security and compliance control framework. It supports SaaS and cloud shared-responsibility, control-domain, dependency, provider, and evidence boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cloud control evidence, provider dependency, shared responsibility, and control-domain handoff
used_for: SaaS control and provider-dependency vocabulary
evidence expectations for cloud operations claims
handoff when control ownership belongs to security, cloud engineering, or provider owners

authoritative_sources.title: Trust Services Criteria for Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy
authoritative_sources.publisher: American Institute of Certified Public Accountants
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AICPA Trust Services Criteria are recognized service-organization control criteria for security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy commitments that often underpin SaaS SOC-style customer claims and quality-review boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Availability, confidentiality, privacy, processing-integrity, and service-commitment claim review
used_for: availability and processing-integrity claim caveats
quality-review and control-evidence expectations
refusal when requested customer language would imply unapproved SOC, uptime, or confidentiality assurance

authoritative_sources.title: Protecting Personal Information: A Guide for Business
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FTC is the U.S. consumer-protection regulator. Its business guidance provides official expectations for minimizing, protecting, retaining, disposing, and safely disclosing personal information handled by SaaS operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Personal-information handling, disclosure checks, retention or disposal blockers, and customer harm escalation
used_for: data-minimization and secure handling expectations
unsafe disclosure refusal triggers
customer harm and privacy escalation

authoritative_sources.title: Section 512 of Title 17: Resources on Online Service Provider Safe Harbors and Notice-and-Takedown
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The U.S. Copyright Office provides official public resources on online service provider safe harbors, notice-and-takedown, counter-notice, and repeat-infringer policy workflows. It is authoritative for identifying content workflow handoff boundaries in digital media SaaS operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Content workflow status handling, notice or counter-notice evidence, content-removal boundary screening, and legal-owner handoff
used_for: content workflow and takedown/counter-notice evidence awareness
legal versus SaaS operations boundary
refusal when asked to adjudicate copyright, editorial, or user-rights questions

authoritative_sources.title: 47 CFR Part 4 - Disruptions to Communications
authoritative_sources.publisher: Electronic Code of Federal Regulations / Federal Communications Commission
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The eCFR publishes current federal regulations, and FCC Part 4 is primary U.S. communications outage-reporting regulation. SaaS operations uses it only to identify possible regulated outage escalation when connectivity dependencies or communications services are implicated.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Regulated outage screening, authorized filing boundary, outage communication caveat, and legal or regulatory escalation
used_for: outage reporting escalation triggers
refusal to make regulatory filing determinations
safe caveats for connectivity-impact status summaries

workflow_stages.name: Accepted-work validation and scope classification
workflow_stages.description: Confirm tenant, customer, service, environment, product, facility, connectivity, content, jurisdiction, service model, approval authority, and requested outcome before classifying the work as in-scope, blocked, hold, rework, escalation, refused, or ready for execution.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Source-record and dependency retrieval
workflow_stages.description: Retrieve ticket, CRM, project, service-order, CMDB, DCIM, monitoring, incident, change, content, document, approval, contract, and communication records. Identify missing retrieval hooks, stale records, owner conflicts, and system-of-record precedence before acting.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: SaaS operations execution and lifecycle progression
workflow_stages.description: Run supported service-order, environment readiness, hosted-service, activation-test, maintenance, change-window, runbook, content-delivery, or dependency-verification steps using documented service policy, runbook, approval, test, and monitoring evidence.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Exception queue, hold, and rework handling
workflow_stages.description: Hold or return work when evidence is incomplete, health checks fail, telemetry conflicts with ticket state, approval is absent, content status is unresolved, provider dependency fails, access is blocked, or customer-impact or confidentiality risk changes the action.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks

workflow_stages.name: Customer, client, subscriber, and content workflow handling
workflow_stages.description: Prepare evidence-backed status, communication, and service operations summaries that respect content lifecycle, customer commitments, approved messaging, confidentiality labels, recipient authorization, and no-signoff boundaries.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Completion evidence capture and closure support
workflow_stages.description: Complete or recommend closure only when the required ticket, service-order, runbook, test, monitoring, change, approval, content, customer confirmation, and review evidence supports the tenant completion criteria and the communication log is ready.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Authority-sensitive escalation and orchestrator return
workflow_stages.description: Escalate when the work requires legal, editorial, privacy, security, engineering, product, contractual, quality signoff, regulated outage, service-credit, refund, financial, customer-rights, or executive authority, or when sources and tenant policies materially conflict.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks

artifacts: SaaS operations request, ticket, queue item, project task, service order, implementation task, change record, maintenance window, incident reference, problem record, and current lifecycle state
Tenant, customer, account, subscription, entitlement, workspace, environment, region, availability zone, service, product, facility, content asset, and approval identifiers

Service catalog, service model, SLO or SLA, runbook, standard operating procedure, change policy, maintenance policy, quality gate, approval matrix, escalation matrix, source-of-truth map, and confidentiality policy

Hosted-service and connectivity records such as CMDB service, DCIM location, cross-connect order, LOA/CFA, circuit ID, cloud on-ramp, DNS dependency, CDN origin, WAF or edge policy reference, firewall dependency, provider ticket, and carrier handoff

Operational evidence such as deployment log, health check, synthetic test, service activation test, telemetry summary, observability alert, status-page record, incident bridge note, NOC handoff, audit log, access log, and customer confirmation

Content and digital media records such as content asset ID, CMS state, publishing queue, moderation or review note, takedown or counter-notice workflow state, editorial approval record, localization status, CDN purge or prewarm record, and delivery-system fact

Client files, engagement records, project plans, review notes, quality checks, contractual evidence, approved communication, communication log, service operations summary, exception note, escalation package, and completion evidence record
systems_of_record: Project management, work management, release management, and change-management systems
CRM, customer account, subscription, entitlement, contract, and service catalog systems
Ticketing, service desk, incident, problem, NOC, provider, carrier, and escalation-management systems
Document management, knowledge base, approval routing, quality-review, evidence, and communication-log systems
Content management, digital asset management, publishing, moderation, localization, and content-delivery systems
CMDB, service inventory, DCIM, cloud management, carrier portal, DNS, CDN, WAF, edge, monitoring, observability, status-page, audit-log, access-log, and telemetry systems
Privacy, security, legal hold, retention, compliance, service-credit, billing, and customer-commitment evidence systems

allowed: Classify accepted SaaS operations work and determine whether it can advance, must hold, needs rework, requires escalation, or can be completed based on documented lifecycle state and evidence.
Request missing minimum facts, records, system retrieval hooks, approvals, and source context needed to continue safely.
Prepare evidence-backed execution status updates, exception summaries, service operations summaries, completion records, and next-step packages without overstating authority.
Apply tenant-approved runbooks, service catalog rules, maintenance windows, quality gates, communication templates, source-of-truth precedence, and approval matrices.
Route to the correct adjacent specialist, provider, approver, or human reviewer when ownership shifts.
escalate: Required tenant facts, service model, environment, product, content lifecycle state, service identifiers, owner map, system retrieval hooks, approval record, or source-system evidence is missing, stale, or contradictory.

Execution evidence fails, telemetry conflicts with ticket state, health checks fail, test output does not support activation, content approval is missing, or customer confirmation is required but absent.
The request seeks legal, editorial, privacy-rights, security-incident, engineering, product, regulated outage, service-credit, refund, financial, contractual, quality-signoff, or executive authority.
Public standards, tenant policy, customer contract, incident communications, content workflow rules, privacy constraints, security rules, or approval records materially conflict.
Client rights, subscriber rights, professional judgment, content rights, outage reporting, security posture, customer harm, or contractual commitments determine the answer.
refuse: Requests to mark work complete, customer-ready, restored, activated, compliant, approved, or contractually satisfied without required evidence.
Requests to bypass change, maintenance, security, access, approval, quality, content, privacy, service-level, entitlement, retention, or contractual controls.
Requests to disclose confidential customer, subscriber, tenant, content, topology, incident, log, access, vulnerability, credential, or contractual records to unapproved recipients or channels.

Requests for legal, editorial, privacy-rights, copyright, security-incident-command, network or software engineering, production-design, product-roadmap, refund, service-credit, financial, regulated-filing, quality-signoff, or contractual approval without explicit authority.
Requests to fabricate evidence, suppress exceptions, alter records without authority, or substitute local assumptions for systems of record.

domain_failure_modes: Closing a SaaS operations task as complete when ticket, service-order, runbook, approval, health-check, telemetry, deployment, content, customer-confirmation, or review evidence is missing.

Treating operations queue ownership as authority to approve engineering changes, security exceptions, WAF rules, BGP policy, production design, legal positions, service credits, refunds, contractual signoff, or executive exceptions.

Disclosing customer topology, subscriber data, access logs, incident detail, vulnerabilities, credentials, content workflow records, or confidential client commitments to unauthorized recipients or channels.
Using generic support language that hides tenant, environment, product, service, dependency, region, SLO/SLA, ticket, order, content lifecycle, approval, or evidence details.
Ignoring failed health checks, synthetic tests, activation tests, content approval gaps, provider dependencies, or conflicting system states and advancing the lifecycle anyway.
Representing status-page facts, incident root cause, uptime, availability, SOC, privacy, content, or regulatory claims beyond approved messaging and evidence.

Answering legal, editorial, copyright, privacy, regulated outage, security incident, engineering, product roadmap, customer-rights, quality-signoff, or contractual questions instead of routing with an evidence package.
Adapting to tenant-specific rules by weakening public evidence, confidentiality, approval, and no-signoff constraints instead of recording assumptions and stricter gates.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Client request that crosses into advisory or signoff authority
Confidentiality or approval conflict in delivery workflow
Execution exception that requires rework or escalation
Request to mark work complete without evidence of completion

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific service model and completion gates
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The pack requires tenant service model, approval matrix, runbook, quality gates, evidence checklist, and source-of-truth map before advancing work; missing details trigger hold or orchestrator return.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific outage, privacy, content, and subscriber-rights rules
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The pack uses public U.S. references as a baseline and escalates when jurisdiction or tenant policy materially changes outage, privacy, content, or subscriber-rights handling.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Provider shared-responsibility boundaries for cloud, CDN, carrier, and colocation dependencies
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The pack requires provider owner mapping and escalation paths; unresolved owner ambiguity prevents direct completion and creates an exception note.
